metadata.title: Claude, On Average
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent; callout ink #161511 on cream; code stays Onda dark glass
metadata.note: Audience-namespaced adaptation. B08 defines when generative variance helps and when AI should not be relied upon; B09 is the audience outro.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Run the same prompt three times; if outputs change, the workflow is sampling a distribution, not retrieving a stored value. The model predicts each next token from prompt, system message, and prior tokens, so context shifts every continuation. Temperature zero narrows variation but does not specify an exact output; personas and context can still change results. Prompts and skills steer a distribution, scripts pin exact behavior, and gates test generated outputs against criteria.

props.artifactTitle: Steer the distribution, pin the exact
artifactLines: Run the same prompt three times; if outputs change, the workflow is sampling a distribution, not retrieving a stored value.
The model predicts each next token from prompt, system message, and prior tokens, so context shifts every continuation.
Temperature zero narrows variation but does not specify an exact output; personas and context can still change results.
Prompts and skills steer a distribution, scripts pin exact behavior, and gates test generated outputs against criteria.

beats.narration_text: The distinction between steering a distribution and pinning exact behavior is what I want to practice. Take an eligibility decision, say approving a refund above a threshold. Can you (1) show me which part of that workflow a prompt or skill can safely steer versus which part must be deterministic code, (2) write the gate that tests a generated output against stated criteria before it is accepted, and (3) explain why running the same prompt three times and comparing is the right first diagnostic here?

props.command: The distinction between steering a distribution and pinning exact behavior is what I want to practice. Take an eligibility decision, say approving a refund above a threshold. Can you (1) show me which part of that workflow a prompt or skill can safely steer versus which part must be deterministic code, (2) write the gate that tests a generated output against stated criteria before it is accepted, and (3) explain why running the same prompt three times and comparing is the right first diagnostic here?

beats.narration_text: Test the behavior directly: run the same prompt three times and compare the outputs. If wording or recommendations change, the workflow is sampling a distribution. A database lookup would return the same stored value.

props.title: same prompt, three draws
props.code:
$ claude -p "write one line about focus"
▸ Focus is the art of saying no.

$ claude -p "write one line about focus"
▸ Attention narrows. That's how you get anywhere.

$ claude -p "write one line about focus"
▸ What you choose not to do is the strategy.

beats.narration_text: Do not cache one generated response as the correct answer. There may be several plausible outputs and no uniquely correct wording. Define what must remain stable, then validate that property separately.

props.heading: Do not cache one draw as the answer
props.body: Several plausible outputs may exist with no uniquely correct wording. Define what must remain stable, then validate that property separately.

beats.narration_text: The model predicts the next token from the prompt, system message, and tokens already generated. It constructs the response sequentially. It does not retrieve a complete stored answer. Context changes the probability of every continuation.

props.heading: Sequential construction from context
props.body: The model predicts the next token from prompt, system message, and tokens already generated. Context changes the probability of every continuation.

beats.narration_text: Temperature zero reduces sampling variation but does not specify an exact output. A changed persona, system message, or preceding context can still change the result. Use low temperature for narrower drafts. Use deterministic code when identical output is required.

props.heading: Low temperature narrows; does not pin
props.body: Changed persona, system message, or context can still change the result at temp zero. Use low temperature for narrower drafts. Use deterministic code when identical output is required.

beats.narration_text: Use variance for brainstorming, alternative framings, and option generation. Sample several outputs and compare them against stated criteria. Do not use creative variance where one uncontrolled difference can change eligibility, payment, safety, or legal treatment.

props.heading: Use variance for options; not for control
props.body: Brainstorming, alternative framings, option generation — sample and compare against stated criteria. Do not use creative variance where one difference changes eligibility, payment, safety, or legal treatment.

beats.narration_text: One draw is insufficient for certification. For factual or high-consequence work, require sources, deterministic calculations, and explicit gates. The model may draft or classify provisionally. A checkable process must approve the result.

props.sparkLine: One draw is not the answer.
props.heading: One draw is not certification
props.body: For factual or high-consequence work: require sources, deterministic calculations, explicit gates. The model may draft or classify provisionally — a checkable process must approve.

beats.narration_text: Operating rule: prompts and skills steer a distribution; scripts pin exact behavior; gates test generated outputs. Choose the mechanism based on the required repeatability and the consequence of error. Never certify a single draw by confidence alone.

artifactLines: You're not querying a database. You're sampling a distribution.
Same prompt, different draw — that's not a bug, that's the definition.
Temperature zero steers toward most likely; it does not specify an output.
Variance is the creativity. Certify draws with gates, not with faith.
Scripts pin the exact. Skills bias the draw. Gates catch the bad draws.

beats.narration_text: Use AI when variability creates value: drafts, options, summaries, and provisional hypotheses. Do not use an unverified draw for payments, eligibility, safety, legal conclusions, or any output that must repeat exactly. Add deterministic checks and a named human approver.

props.artifactTitle: When variation helps — and when it harms
artifactLines: USE — options · drafts · summaries · provisional hypotheses
DO NOT USE UNVERIFIED — payment · eligibility · safety · legal decisions
Controls — deterministic gate · sources · named approver

cli_exercise.ask: claude "Run this prompt five times. Build a comparison table of claims, recommendations, and wording that changed. Flag every field that must be deterministic in production."
cli_exercise.output_description: A five-run variance table separating stable claims from unstable wording and recommendations.
cli_exercise.change: Add consequence-of-error and required-control columns; mark high-consequence unstable fields as blocked.
cli_exercise.output2_description: A deployment gate table showing which fields need sources, code, or human approval.
cli_exercise.next_step: Run it on your own production prompt and real acceptance criteria.

beats.narration_text: Apply the method with Humanitarians AI. More at humanitarians.ai.
